System Tutorial  
This page is intended to provide a reference for the features of sprites in the  
TFBTool (with Madagascar 2 in mind), as well as inform designers about  
making good decisions in the way they put their sprites together.

Setting up Sprites  
Sprite Types  
Art  
Art sprites will be their true pixel dimensions on screen unless you check the force size checkbox to  
deﬁne dimensions.  
File: Pick a .png ﬁle.  
Tile: If the force size box is checked, checking this box will cause the sprite to tile repeatedly  
within the dimensions speciﬁed.  
Text  
1 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
Text sprites will justify with the alignment of a sprite (so an Upper-Left aligned sprite will have text that  
justiﬁes left; and a Bottom-Right aligned sprite, right), unless the text is checked to wrap, in which case  
the Left/Center/Right ﬂags deﬁne how it justiﬁes within the box.  
String: This will reference a string deﬁned in your level or an included level. Information on String  
Creation can be found here.  
Font: Pick a font .png ﬁle.  
Wrap/shrink options - These rely on setting a forced width/height. See below for what these  
parameters mean.  
If Wrap text is checked: This will wrap your text within the width deﬁned in the size section.  
If Shrink Only is checked: Your text will shrink to ﬁt within the width deﬁned in the size  
section.  
If both Wrap Text and Shrink Only are checked: Your text will wrap to ﬁt within the width  
deﬁned, but if, in the process of wrapping, it continues past the height deﬁned, it will shrink  
your text horizontally to ﬁt precisely within the dimensions you specify.  
Text sprites also allow you to set the data value ﬁeld, which will change the number displayed if  
you're using a string that has a place for a number.  
See the below examples/suggestions regarding the various text options at your disposal. The red box  
shows the size you've speciﬁed in the width/height area of the sprite when force size is enabled.  
2 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
3D  
Model: Select a .mdl to be your 3D sprite.  
Container  
Container sprites serve to facilitate manipulating or organizing multiple sprites at once. Their use will  
be more clear upon reading the section on force size, alignment, and parenting, but you can think of  
them as invisible 'art' sprites that you can use as parents to lay out other sprites.  
Placement  
X/Y: Setting a sprite's X and Y coordinates will move it so many pixels on the X and Y from its  
parent.  
Priority: Priority ranges from -200 to 200. Lower numbers are pulled closer to the screen and will  
appear in front of those with higher numbers.  
3 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
Orientation  
Facing: A sprite's main 2D rotation is set via facing (an easy way to imagine it is that you're  
looking at an actor from the top down).  
Tilt: Tilt will pitch your sprite, again, as if you were looking from the top down.  
Roll: Roll will pitch your sprite sideways, yet again, as if you were looking from the top down.  
NOTE: Sprites are currently all rendered orthogonally, so you actually wouldn't be able to mimic  
this eﬀect with a 2D sprite. It's just done this way to illustrate the eﬀect it will have in sprite space.  
Force Size  
If you check the force size checkbox, the width, height, and depth parameters will become available.  
Checking this box has diﬀerent eﬀects depending on the type of sprite deﬁned, but, in general, the idea  
is that you are trying to make the sprite take up ﬁxed screen space (so many pixels wide, so many pixels  
high).  
For an art sprite: If unchecked, the sprite will be its true dimensions (deﬁned in the .png ﬁle), but,  
if checked, it will stretch/squash to ﬁll the dimensions speciﬁed.  
For a text sprite: When checked, this has many uses, and I suggest you see the prior text section  
for information.  
For a 3D sprite: Similar to the art sprite, when checked, the sprite will ﬁll your dimensions when  
looking from the front. That is, by tilting/rolling the sprite, you may cause it to actually take up  
more screen space as we see the rear of the model come into view.  
For a container sprite: Force size is a requirement for this type of sprite to give it container-like  
dimensions. Without it, it's just a 'point,' though that may prove useful for some of your sprite  
hierarchies.  
Alignment  
You can imagine alignment as a 3x3 grid (9 total points) on a sprite that allows you to deﬁne its center.  
4 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
If you set a sprite to be at (0,0) with an alignment of Upper-Left, the top left corner of the sprite will be  
at (0,0). All operations that modify the sprite, such as location, scale, and rotation, will center around  
the alignment point. Note how changing the alignment changes the look of scaling and rotating sprites.  
Parenting  
Basics  
The primary function of parenting a sprite to another is to inherit many properties of its parent and  
move/rotate/etc. along with it.  
These properties are inherited:  
Location  
Scale  
Orientation  
Alpha (but not R/G/B colors)  
Priority  
Visibility  
For the following example, let's assume Sprite B is attached to Sprite A  
5 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
Align To Parent  
Align to parent is a way of picking one of the 9 alignment points for your sprite to stick to. By default, it  
aligns to the 'Upper-Left' point (like parenting in the old TFBTool), but if you pick, for example, 'Upper-  
Right,' it will position its center (deﬁned by its alignment) at the upper right corner of the parent. This  
corner is deﬁned by the sprite's dimensions and behaves just like the alignment diagram above.  
6 of 9

http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
Align to Parent Container Example  
With the concept of align to parent, container sprites get to be very interesting. You can create UI  
layouts within those containers, and, by redimensioning the containers, move elements around because  
they are aligned to diﬀerent sections of the container. For example, it would be very useful for a  
multiplayer UI:  
7 of 9  
01/11/2016 03:28 PM  
Sprite System Tutorial - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Sprite_System_Tutorial  
Parent=<NONE>  
Sprites, by default, have a parent of <NONE>, but that actually means that it's parented to the  
viewport's container sprite. Thus, if a sprite has no parent, and its align to parent is set to Middle-  
Center, it will always be in the center of the screen if its position is (0,0).

When you need absolute screen coordinates in HD  
TODO  
Mad2 Hi Def and Safe Zone Compliance  
Safe Zone  
There is a sprite called _Gameplay_Container in global.lvl. For all instances when you would  
parent something to the screen container (<NONE>), use that one instead, as it will be adjusted to  
comply with all our platforms' safe zones and hide upon pausing the game.  
If you don't want a sprite to hide during pause, attach it to _UI_Container in global.lvl, and have  
a good reason for doing this!  
Hi Def  
This should be made very, very clear:  
Putting a sprite at X=320 and Y=240 WILL NOT guarantee that it's in the center of the  
screen.  
Putting a sprite at X=640 and Y=480 with an alignment of bottom right WILL NOT guarantee  
that it's in the bottom right corner of the screen.  
Putting a sprite at X=800 WILL NOT guarantee that it's actually oﬀ-screen. (Use the  
Visibility ﬂag instead!)  
HD displays have diﬀerent display resolutions (e.g. 1280x720, not the 640x480 we're used to), so  
those pixel oﬀsets don't make sense anymore. You should make use of the Align to Parent  
techniques to place your sprites in the appropriate place on screen.  
When designing your UI or trying to ﬁgure out how to apply this concept, consider what  
region of the screen you want your sprites to occupy. When the screen expands to HD, what is  
the relevant edge/corner of the screen that it should follow?
